g.data-narration: Welcome! Today we explore two fundamental memory allocation strategies that form the backbone of data structures.
g.text: Understanding Memory Management in Data Structures

g.data-narration: The choice between static and linked allocation impacts performance, memory usage, and flexibility in every program you write.
g.text: Understanding memory allocation is fundamental to writing efficient software.
Your choice affects: Speed | Memory Usage | Flexibility

g.data-narration: We study two main approaches: Static Allocation uses fixed-size arrays, while Linked Allocation uses dynamic nodes connected by pointers.
In static allocation, memory is reserved at compile time. The array occupies a contiguous block - elements are accessed using base address and index.
Linked allocation creates nodes dynamically. Each node holds data and a pointer to the next node. Memory is allocated one piece at a time.
Memory layout differs significantly: static arrays need one large contiguous block, while linked structures scatter nodes across the heap.
g.text: Static: One large continuous block needed
Linked: Small scattered chunks connected by pointers
Static may fail if no contiguous space available

g.data-narration: Access time differs: arrays provide O(1) constant-time random access, while linked lists require O(n) sequential traversal.
Insertion and deletion operations favor linked lists: O(1) by pointer manipulation versus O(n) for arrays requiring element shifting.
Memory efficiency differs: arrays may waste space with over-allocation or overflow with under-allocation, while linked lists use exactly what's needed plus pointer overhead.
Choose static allocation for fixed-size, frequent random access needs. Choose linked allocation for dynamic size and frequent insertions/deletions.
g.text: Size is fixed • Frequent random access • Cache performance matters
Size varies • Frequent insert/delete • Unknown size at compile time
Array: Leaderboards | Linked: Browser history, Playlists

g.data-narration: Let's review the key differences in a comprehensive comparison table covering all major aspects of both allocation strategies.
In summary: static allocation offers speed and cache efficiency but lacks flexibility, while linked allocation provides dynamic growth at the cost of extra memory and slower access.
g.text: Know size? → Array | Size changes? → Linked List
Frequent access? → Array | Frequent insert/delete? → Linked List
